questions.prompt: State the NPV decision rule for a single project:
choices: Take the project if its IRR exceeds the historical average return on stocks
Take the project if PV(benefits) exceeds PV(costs); equivalently if NPV > 0
Take the project if its payback period is under three years
Take the project only if it has positive expected accounting profit
questions.explanation: NPV > 0 means the project creates more value than it costs at the market discount rate. The other rules have failure modes covered in the next lesson.

questions.prompt: The Law of One Price says:
choices: Every asset must have the same price
Two assets that produce identical cash flows in all states must have identical prices
Stocks and bonds must be equally priced
questions.explanation: The Law of One Price is cash-flow-specific. Different cash flow patterns can have different prices; identical cash flows cannot, because arbitrageurs would close the gap.

questions.prompt: A one-year risk-free bond paying $100 trades at $95.24 (yielding ~5%). A one-year credit-default-swap that pays $100 in case of default trades at $4 today (you receive the $4 premium upfront). What is the no-arbitrage price for a one-year corporate bond that pays $100 if the firm survives and $0 if it defaults? Answer in dollars to two decimals.
questions.explanation: Replicate the risky bond as: long the risk-free bond (cost $95.24) + short the CDS (receive $4). Net cost = $95.24 − $4 = $91.24. If the risky bond trades for anything else, arbitrage exists.

questions.prompt: EUR/USD = 1.10, USD/JPY = 110. What should EUR/JPY be (approximately) to avoid triangular arbitrage?
questions.explanation: 1 EUR × 1.10 USD/EUR × 110 JPY/USD = 121 JPY. Any other cross rate enables triangular arbitrage.

furtherReading.title: Hedge funds at the heart of Treasury market turmoil as basis trades unwind
furtherReading.why: April 2025 Treasury cash-futures basis blowout — a textbook real-world breakdown of the law of one price when leveraged arbitrageurs are forced to unwind.
